provider.name: Coastal Elements AI (with poly186.io as technical/delivery partner)

contacts.name: Dylan Ryan
contacts.notes: Primary business and account lead; based in Virginia Beach / Hampton Roads. Will schedule follow‑ups and send workshop materials.

contacts.name: Princeps Polycap
contacts.notes: Leads AI/LLM architecture, infrastructure, model selection, and implementation; focuses on Azure/OpenAI/Claude, process automation, and fine‑tuning.

contacts.name: Drew (last name not provided)
contacts.notes: Coordinates teams, timelines, resources, and client communications for implementations.

contacts.name: Richard
contacts.notes: Existing relationship with Dylan and Mark; made the introduction between Coastal Elements AI and S.L. Nusbaum; will likely be a stakeholder among brokers in future workshops.

client.name: S.L. Nusbaum (also referred to as SL Nesbaum / Nesbom)

contacts.name: Mark Ashley
contacts.notes: Primary client sponsor and decision maker for AI/IT initiatives; manages small IT team supporting ~200 corporate and ~1,000 multifamily employees; wants governance, Azure‑native solutions, and marketable AI offerings for clients.

deliverables: Initial AI readiness and process/data audit focusing on key departments (e.g., HR, Finance/Accounting, Multifamily Operations, Brokerage).
Workshop framework document outlining how to run AI discovery sessions with department heads (including agendas and facilitation approach).
Department‑specific discovery question lists (e.g., for HR, Finance/Accounting, Multifamily properties, Brokerage) to uncover AI/automation use cases and pain points.
ROI-oriented analysis template for AI use cases (time spent, manual effort, process fragmentation, expected savings/value).
Recommendation summary of potential AI use cases, prioritized by ROI and strategic value (especially client‑facing, marketable offerings).

Design and potential build of AI solutions in S.L. Nusbaum’s Azure environment (e.g., contract review assistant, process automation, fine‑tuned models on Nusbaum contracts/data) after January follow‑up and scoping.
Optionally, access/integration to Coastal Elements’ existing real‑estate AI SaaS solutions, where appropriate, instead of custom “snowflake” builds.

phases.name: Phase 1 – Discovery & AI Readiness
phases.description: Understand S.L. Nusbaum’s current AI usage, infrastructure (Azure/365/Copilot), departmental needs, and executive priorities; identify quick wins and strategically valuable client‑facing opportunities.
activities: Conduct high‑level AI/process/data audit (focusing on current Azure, M365/Copilot, existing AI bots such as utility bill processing).
Hold one‑on‑one discussions with executives and department heads (HR, Finance/Accounting, Multifamily, Brokerage) using provided workshop framework and questions.
Map existing KPIs/OKRs to potential AI/automation improvements (time savings, error reduction, process throughput).
Identify major “time sucks” and manual/administrative tasks suitable for automation (document/contract review, data aggregation, reporting).
dependencies: Mark completes internal conversations with executives, department heads, and brokers (Nov–Jan).
Workshop question framework and guidance from Coastal Elements AI provided to Mark.
outputs: List of departmental pain points and AI/automation opportunities.
Preliminary inventory of candidate use cases (categorized by process‑heavy, documentation‑heavy, client‑facing, etc.).
Baseline data for ROI modeling (current time/effort vs expected post‑automation).

phases.name: Phase 2 – Use Case Prioritization & POC Design
phases.description: Translate discovery findings into prioritized AI initiatives and define one or more POCs, with clear ROI and governance.
activities: Evaluate identified use cases against KPIs/OKRs, ROI potential, and strategic value (especially offerings that can be marketed to Nusbaum’s clients).
Decide whether to leverage existing vendor AI features, Coastal Elements SaaS tools, or net‑new custom builds in Azure.
Design one or more POCs (e.g., contract redlining assistant, broker “easy button” reports, multifamily/utility‑like automations) including data sources, models, and security constraints.
Estimate costs, resource needs, and timelines for POCs; define success metrics and governance requirements.
dependencies: Executive clarity on which types of AI solutions they want to take to market (client‑facing vs purely internal).
outputs: Prioritized AI use case roadmap (short‑, medium‑, and long‑term).
POC design documents (architecture, data flows, model choices, integration points, security/governance approach).
Initial budgetary estimates and ROI justification narrative for executive committee.

phases.name: Phase 3 – POC Build & Deployment (Client Environment)
phases.description: Implement selected AI POC(s) primarily within Nusbaum’s Azure tenant, integrating with existing systems (M365, line‑of‑business applications, SharePoint).
activities: Stand up required Azure services and AI infrastructure (OpenAI/LLMs, storage, APIs) within Nusbaum’s Azure environment.
Ingest and preprocess relevant internal documents/data (e.g., contracts, SOPs, utility bills, property data) for use by LLMs with appropriate access controls.
Build AI automations and/or agents (e.g., contract redline assistant, broker briefing “easy button” generator, process bots to bridge fragmented workflows).
Fine‑tune or configure models based on Nusbaum’s contracts and internal documents where beneficial.
User acceptance testing with targeted user groups (brokers, admins, accounting, etc.), iterate on prompts/system instructions to reduce hallucinations and ensure usability.
Handover documentation and light training for IT and end users.
dependencies: Green‑light and budget approval from executive committee based on Phase 2 outputs.
Azure access and security/governance approvals from Mark and IT team.
outputs: Working POC(s) deployed in Nusbaum’s Azure environment or via shared environment if POC hosted by Coastal initially.
Usage and performance metrics against defined KPIs/OKRs.
Refined rollout and scale‑up plan if POC is successful.

phases.name: Phase 4 – Scale‑up & Marketable Offering
phases.description: Scale successful POCs into production and position them as part of Nusbaum’s client‑facing offerings.
activities: Harden POC solutions for production (monitoring, logging, support processes).
Expand rollout across departments/properties where applicable (commercial, multifamily, nursing homes, etc.).
Assist in articulating AI offerings for Nusbaum’s marketing and sales (e.g., how AI supports $100–300M deals, operational excellence).
Ongoing advisory to keep solutions forward‑looking, leveraging new tools while avoiding new “snowflake” tech debt.
dependencies: Successful POC(s) with demonstrated ROI and executive sponsorship.
Defined go‑to‑market message for AI‑enabled services.
outputs: Production AI solutions embedded in Nusbaum’s operations and client offerings.
Executive‑level AI value story and collateral (high‑level description, capabilities, and client benefits).

timeline.duration: Exploratory phase through mid‑January 2026, with POC and implementation timelines to be determined after discovery.

milestones.name: Internal Discovery by Mark with Execs & Departments
milestones.targetDate: By mid‑January (per Mark: department head meeting later this month; broker meeting in January)
milestones.details: Mark to meet with executives, department heads, and brokers to gather AI needs, pain points, and opportunities.

milestones.name: Mid‑January Follow‑Up Meeting
milestones.details: Regroup between Coastal Elements AI and S.L. Nusbaum to review discovery insights, discuss opportunities, and identify candidate POCs and partnership model.

milestones.name: Workshop Framework & Questions Delivery
milestones.targetDate: Within ~1 week after the November 5 meeting
milestones.details: Dylan to email Mark example workshop framework and department‑specific discovery questions to guide internal conversations.

milestones.name: Potential POC Kickoff
milestones.details: To be scheduled post mid‑January meeting, once prioritized use cases and budget are defined.

commercialTerms.roiExpectation: Engagements must show clear ROI beyond “AI for AI’s sake”, especially for executive approval. ROI will primarily be demonstrated via time savings on manual/administrative tasks (e.g., document review, data aggregation), improved process efficiency, and creation of differentiated, client‑facing AI offerings that brokers and executives can market in large ($100–300M) deals. Coastal will help quantify ROI by comparing current manual time vs. post‑automation time and tying improvements to existing KPIs/OKRs.

nextSteps.action: Schedule a mid‑January follow‑up meeting with Mark to review departmental conversations and AI opportunities.
nextSteps.source: “maybe mid-January, kind of regroup, and see what conversations we're starting to have, and see if there's an opportunity, and just go from there.”

nextSteps.action: Email Mark a workshop framework plus discovery questions for key departments (HR, Finance/Accounting, Multifamily, Brokerage) to use in upcoming internal meetings.
nextSteps.source: “I'm going to... put together a couple things just to send over, like, hey, you know, this is an example of, like, what a workshop would look like, a framework there, maybe some, you know, questions that might be good from certain departments...”

nextSteps.action: Conduct one‑on‑one discussions with executives and department heads to understand their AI needs and ideas, including department head meeting this month and broker meeting in January.
nextSteps.source: “I'm starting to have more conversations with some of the department heads, and we have a department head meeting later this month... some of the brokers, they have a monthly meeting that they invited me to in January.”

nextSteps.action: Clarify executive committee expectations for AI (especially client‑facing offerings that can be marketed in large deals) and identify potential high‑value use cases.
nextSteps.source: “it needs to be something that they can go out there in the field, when they're meeting with people, and they're talking about, you know, $100, $200, $300 million deal, and, hey, by the way, this is part of our offering...”

nextSteps.action: Schedule an informal lunch/beer meeting before January follow‑up to strengthen relationship and potentially discuss AI ideas informally.
nextSteps.source: “We’ll grab lunch one day, maybe a late lunch, and share a beer... I'm genuinely going to reach out, see if can grab lunch or something here, even before we meet next.”

keyQuotes: “My focus has been, you know, we have Copilot, we've released it to everyone, you know, that there's your assistant, right?... And then when it comes to specific requests, my first question is, you know, the solution you're looking for within this application or offering whatever, does the vendor have something? Because my goal is to really force the vendors into offering this stuff, rather than getting into the trap... where there's a bunch of homebrew, one-off, you know, snowflake solutions.”

“To me, it is what, like, at the most simplistic level... I tend to look at what time is being spent, right?... that's one of the biggest things that we do on the front end of any sort of [engagement]... because part of the challenge is exactly that, ROI. What is the ROI that you're going to get from that?... We can do that... the amount of time spent by resources doing this versus the amount of time now being spent on that same activity, that shows an ROI there immediately.”

“I really think that, you know, a partnership between our groups, I see it being more of a, you know, more focusing on developing a solution, not so much answering a problem we have... I think what you're going to start seeing is companies like ours really starting to market what AI solutions they have and how, you know, how we're offering AI to benefit our customers. And if we're not doing that, then I think that it's going to pass us by.”
